section.title: The Brothers Go to Egypt
section.sectionRationale: The famine that Joseph foresaw now drives his brothers to the very man they betrayed. Without knowing it, they bow before the brother they sold, and his dreams begin to come true. The chapter turns from grain to guilt, as an old sin surfaces in a foreign court.
section.thesis: God uses famine and a hidden ruler to bring the guilty brothers low, awakening their consciences and setting in motion the reconciliation that will save the family alive.
section.frameNote: Four scenes: Jacob sends his sons for grain; they bow before Joseph and are accused; a buried guilt is awakened; and dread deepens as the silver is found and Jacob refuses to risk Benjamin.

units.label: Sent down to Egypt
units.note: so that we may live and not die

verses.text: When Jacob learned that there was grain in Egypt, he said to his sons, “Why are you staring at one another?”
“Look,” he added, “I have heard that there is grain in Egypt. Go down there and buy some for us, so that we may live and not die.”
So ten of Joseph’s brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.
But Jacob did not send Joseph’s brother Benjamin with his brothers, for he said, “I am afraid that harm might befall him.”
So the sons of Israel were among those who came to buy grain, since the famine had also spread to the land of Canaan.

units.label: Bowing before the brother they sold
units.note: Joseph's dreams begin to come true

verses.text: Now Joseph was the ruler of the land; he was the one who sold grain to all its people. So when his brothers arrived, they bowed down before him with their faces to the ground.

And when Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them, but he treated them as strangers and spoke harshly to them. “Where have you come from?” he asked. “From the land of Canaan,” they replied. “We are here to buy food.”
Although Joseph recognized his brothers, they did not recognize him.
Joseph remembered his dreams about them and said, “You are {{ragal|spies}}! You have come to see if our land is vulnerable.”
“Not so, my lord,” they replied. “Your servants have come to buy food.
We are all sons of one man. Your servants are honest men, not spies.”
“No,” he told them. “You have come to see if our land is vulnerable.”
But they answered, “Your servants are twelve brothers, the sons of one man in the land of Canaan. The youngest is now with our father, and one is no more.”
Then Joseph declared, “Just as I said, you are spies!
And this is how you will be {{bachan|tested}}: As surely as Pharaoh lives, you shall not leave this place unless your youngest brother comes here.
Send one of your number to get your brother; the rest of you will be confined so that the truth of your words may be tested. If they are untrue, then as surely as Pharaoh lives, you are spies!”
So Joseph imprisoned them for three days,

units.label: A guilty conscience awakened
units.note: surely we are being punished because of our brother

verses.text: and on the third day he said to them, “I fear God. So do this and you will live:
If you are honest, leave one of your brothers in custody while the rest of you go and take back grain to relieve the hunger of your households.
Then bring your youngest brother to me so that your words can be verified, that you may not die.” And to this they consented.
Then they said to one another, “Surely we are being punished because of our brother. We saw his anguish when he pleaded with us, but we would not listen. That is why this distress has come upon us.”
And Reuben responded, “Didn’t I tell you not to sin against the boy? But you would not listen. Now we must account for his blood!”
They did not realize that Joseph understood them, since there was an interpreter between them.
And he turned away from them and wept. When he turned back and spoke to them, he took Simeon from them and had him bound before their eyes.
Then Joseph gave orders to fill their bags with grain, to return each man’s silver to his sack, and to give them provisions for their journey. This order was carried out,
and they loaded the grain on their donkeys and departed.
At the place where they lodged for the night, one of them opened his sack to get feed for his donkey, and he saw his silver in the mouth of the sack.
“My silver has been returned!” he said to his brothers. “It is here in my sack.” Their hearts sank, and trembling, they turned to one another and said, “What is this that God has done to us?”
When they reached their father Jacob in the land of Canaan, they described to him all that had happened to them:
“The man who is lord of the land spoke harshly to us and accused us of spying on the country.
But we told him, ‘We are honest men, not spies.
We are twelve brothers, sons of one father. One is no more, and the youngest is now with our father in the land of Canaan.’
Then the man who is lord of the land said to us, ‘This is how I will know whether you are honest: Leave one brother with me, take food to relieve the hunger of your households, and go.
But bring your youngest brother back to me so I will know that you are not spies but honest men. Then I will give your brother back to you, and you can trade in the land.’”
As they began emptying their sacks, there in each man’s sack was his bag of silver! And when they and their father saw the bags of silver, they were dismayed.
Their father Jacob said to them, “You have deprived me of my sons. Joseph is gone and Simeon is no more. Now you want to take Benjamin. Everything is going against me!”
Then Reuben said to his father, “You may kill my two sons if I fail to bring him back to you. Put him in my care, and I will return him.”

But Jacob replied, “My son will not go down there with you, for his brother is dead, and he alone is left. If any harm comes to him on your journey, you will bring my gray hair down to {{sheol|Sheol}} in sorrow.”

ragal.gloss: To go about on foot, and so to spy out or scout. The accusation that they are spies is heavy with irony, for these men once schemed in secret against their own brother. Joseph presses the charge not out of cruelty but to test whether they have changed.

bachan.gloss: To test, examine, or prove, often as metal is assayed by fire. Joseph puts his brothers to the proof to see what is truly in their hearts. Scripture uses the same word for how God tests the righteous to reveal and refine them.

sheol.gloss: The grave or the realm of the dead, the place to which all the living descend. Jacob fears that losing Benjamin will send him grieving to Sheol. His words expose a father whose whole hope now rests on one remaining son of Rachel.

basic.context: The famine Joseph predicted has reached Canaan, so Jacob sends ten sons to buy grain in Egypt. There they bow before the governor without knowing he is the brother they sold years before. The key question is whether God is simply punishing them or quietly working to restore them.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative, and it moves by dramatic irony: we know who Joseph is, but the brothers do not. The main question is why Joseph tests them so severely rather than revealing himself at once. The answer unfolds as we watch old guilt rise to the surface.
basic.originalLanguages: The charge that they are spies, from a word for scouting on foot, is loaded with irony against men who once plotted in secret. Joseph tests them, a word used for assaying metal, to see their hearts. Jacob speaks of going down to Sheol, the grave, if he loses another son.
basic.typology: Joseph, the rejected brother now raised to power, holds the fate of those who wronged him and yet weeps for them. This points forward to Christ, the brother His own people rejected, exalted to hold the bread of life and moved with compassion for the guilty.
basic.christ: Joseph turns aside to weep even while he tests them, and he is the only source of grain in the land. So Jesus, rejected by His brothers, becomes the one to whom all must come for bread, and He receives sinners with tears rather than vengeance.
basic.god: God is at work behind the famine, the journey, and even the returned silver. The brothers sense His hand and ask, what is this that God has done to us. He is bringing hidden sin into the light so that it can finally be healed.
basic.crossReferences: Their bowing fulfills Joseph's dreams in Genesis 37. Their awakened guilt echoes the sin recorded there, and Joseph's later words in Genesis 50:20 interpret the whole account: what they meant for evil, God meant for good.
basic.oneStory: This is the beginning of the family's rescue. God preserves the line of promise through famine by the very brother they tried to destroy, keeping alive the people from whom the Saviour will come.

context.literary: Part of the Joseph narrative, the longest sustained story in Genesis. The chapter is built on dramatic irony and repeated journeys, and it deliberately echoes Genesis 37, where the brothers first sinned. Their bowing is the first fulfillment of Joseph's boyhood dreams.
context.historical: Egypt's grain reserves under a centralized administration made it the refuge of surrounding peoples during regional famine. Foreigners buying food would be interrogated at the border, and an interpreter between Hebrew and Egyptian is historically fitting.
context.canonical: This movement advances the covenant promise to Abraham. The survival of Jacob's household in famine keeps alive the nation from which Messiah will come, and it sets the stage for the descent into Egypt that Exodus will later reverse.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show God sovereignly bringing the guilty brothers to a reckoning, fulfilling Joseph's dreams, and beginning the long road to reconciliation. The narrator wants us to see providence at work in ordinary events like hunger and trade.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The account describes Joseph's testing of his brothers; it does not prescribe elaborate schemes as a model for handling those who wrong us. The abiding truths are God's providence, the awakening of conscience, and the patience that seeks true repentance rather than mere punishment.

debates.question: Why does Joseph conceal his identity and deal harshly with his brothers?

views.label: Testing toward repentance
views.case: Joseph is probing whether they have changed, especially toward Benjamin, the other son of Rachel; his tears show love, not revenge.

views.label: Working through his own pain
views.case: Some see Joseph also wrestling with old wounds, taking time before he can trust and forgive; the harshness reflects a real, unresolved history.

debates.bearing: Either way the goal is restoration, not retaliation. The text presents Joseph's severity as a means to genuine reconciliation, since he weeps rather than gloats.

study.originalLanguages: Joseph accuses them of being spies, from a verb meaning to scout on foot, a pointed irony against men who once conspired in secret. He says they will be tested, a word for assaying metal in the fire, for he wants to know their hearts, not merely their words. The brothers confess, in Hebrew, that this distress has come because of their brother, and Jacob dreads descending in sorrow to Sheol, the grave.

typology.type: The rejected brother raised to rule
typology.fulfillment: Christ, rejected by His own, exalted to the right hand of power, holding the fate of those who wronged Him.

typology.type: The sole keeper of the grain
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the bread of life, to whom all must come to live and not die.
Christ's compassion for sinners, weeping over the city and over the dead rather than condemning at once.

study.christ: Joseph is the brother his family rejected, now exalted, holding power to punish, yet he turns aside to weep. He alone dispenses the grain that keeps the world alive. In this he foreshadows Jesus, despised and handed over by His own, raised to the throne, and become the only bread by which sinners live. As Joseph tests in order to restore, Christ deals with sinners not to destroy but to bring them to repentance and life.
study.god: God stands behind the whole scene: the famine that drives them, the ruler who receives them, and the silver that unsettles them. The brothers rightly ask, what is this that God has done to us, sensing a hand they cannot yet read. He is just, letting their sin catch up with them, and merciful, steering that guilt toward healing rather than ruin.

crossReferences.note: Joseph's dreams of his brothers bowing, now beginning to be fulfilled.
The original sale of Joseph, the guilt now surfacing in Egypt.
Joseph's later interpretation: what they meant for evil, God meant for good.
Be sure your sin will find you out, echoed as the brothers' guilt rises.

study.oneStory: The famine that could have ended the family instead drives it toward rescue. God preserves the covenant line through the very brother they meant to destroy, and He begins to expose and heal an old sin. The people from whom Messiah will come are kept alive, and the road toward reconciliation, and toward Egypt and a later Exodus, opens here.

quiz.q: What happens when the brothers arrive before the governor of Egypt?

options.text: They bow with their faces to the ground, unknowingly fulfilling Joseph's dreams.
options.note: Their bowing fulfills the very dreams they once despised in Genesis 37.

options.text: They recognize Joseph immediately and beg for mercy.
options.note: They do bow, but they do not recognize him; only Joseph knows who they are.

options.text: They are welcomed as honored guests and given a feast.
options.note: That comes later; here Joseph treats them as strangers and accuses them.

options.text: They are turned away without any grain.
options.note: They are not turned away; they are tested and eventually sent home with grain.

quiz.q: Why does Joseph accuse his brothers of being spies and test them?

options.text: To prove their hearts and see whether they have truly changed.
options.note: The testing aims at repentance and restoration, shown by his weeping.

options.text: Mainly to make them suffer as he had suffered.
options.note: There is real pain, but the account moves toward reconciliation, not revenge.

options.text: Because he genuinely believes they are enemy spies.
options.note: He knows exactly who they are; the charge is a deliberate test.

options.text: Because God commanded him to punish them.
options.note: No such command is given; Joseph acts to test and eventually restore them.

quiz.q: How do the brothers interpret the trouble that comes upon them?

options.text: As a reckoning for what they did to their brother long ago.
options.note: Their conscience awakens: surely we are being punished because of our brother.

options.text: As random misfortune from a cruel Egyptian ruler.
options.note: They do feel mistreated, but they trace it back to their own guilt.

options.text: As a test of their trading skills.
options.note: They see something far deeper than commerce, namely God's hand on their sin.

options.text: As proof that they had done nothing wrong.
options.note: The opposite; they openly confess their old guilt to one another.

quiz.q: How is God at work in this chapter?

options.text: Through famine, the journey, and the returned silver, He brings hidden sin to light for healing.
options.note: The brothers sense it: what is this that God has done to us.

options.text: He is mostly absent, letting human schemes run their course.
options.note: God is not named on every line, but His providence clearly drives events.

options.text: He works only to punish and destroy the guilty.
options.note: His aim is restoration, not destruction, as the story will show.

options.text: He has abandoned the family to famine.
options.note: The famine is the very means by which He preserves them alive.

quiz.q: Why does Jacob refuse to send Benjamin to Egypt?

options.text: He fears losing the last son of Rachel and being brought in sorrow to the grave.
options.note: Benjamin is all he feels he has left of Joseph's mother.

options.text: He distrusts the Egyptian ruler's motives entirely.
options.note: There is suspicion, but his deepest reason is grief and fear of loss.

options.text: He believes Simeon will soon be released anyway.
options.note: He shows little hope for Simeon; his focus is on protecting Benjamin.

options.text: He thinks Benjamin is too young to travel.
options.note: The issue is not age but Jacob's fear of losing him as he lost Joseph.

questions: The brothers' old sin surfaced years later in a foreign land. Is there something in your past that God is gently bringing back to mind so it can finally be dealt with?
Joseph tested his brothers to see if they had changed. How can we tell the difference between real repentance and simply being sorry we were caught?
The brothers asked, what is this that God has done to us. Where might God be at work in a hard circumstance you are facing right now?
Joseph held power over those who wronged him yet turned aside to weep. How does his response challenge the way you treat people who have hurt you?
Jacob was gripped by fear of losing Benjamin. Where is fear of loss keeping you from trusting God with someone or something you love?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered silver can also mean money; the same term runs through chapters 42 to 44 as the returned silver keeps unsettling the brothers.
Sheol is the grave or realm of the dead; Jacob pictures his grief carrying him down there, a vivid image of a sorrow he feels he could not survive.
